problem_summary: 2016 MCM Problem A (continuous): model the temperature of bathtub water in space and time, propose an optimal strategy for the bather to keep water temperature even and close to the initial temperature while minimizing wasted water, and assess sensitivity to tub shape/volume, the person (shape/volume/temperature/motion), and bubble-bath additives. This O-award paper frames the tub as a thermodynamic control volume and compares two refill strategies (continuous trickle vs. discontinuous batch reheating), recommending continuous low mass-flow inflow (≈0.03–0.16 kg/s depending on conditions) as the water-saving optimum.
models_used: Convection heat-transfer governing equations in Cartesian coordinates: continuity, steady Navier-Stokes momentum equations (3 components, with gravity in z), and the energy/temperature-convection-diffusion equation — set up as the spatial temperature field PDE system
Newton's law of cooling for the free top surface heat flux: Phi_1 = h*A*(t_f - t_inf)

Series-thermal-resistance (composite wall) conduction model for the insulated tub walls: Phi_2 = (t_f - t_inf) / [(1/A)(1/h1 + delta1/k1 + delta2/k2 + 1/h2)] — convection + two insulation layers + outer convection in series
Sub-model I (continuous inflow): steady-state heat-balance / enthalpy model q_m*c_p*Δt = Q with mean temperature t_f = (t_in + t_out)/2 to solve for the required mass flow

Sub-model II (discontinuous inflow): an air-conditioner-inspired two-phase iteration of a HEATING phase (unsteady N-S + unsteady energy equation, energy conservation Q = Q_in + Q_out to find inflow time T1 and mass flow q_m) and a STANDBY phase (lumped-capacitance Newton cooling ODE -rho*c_p*V dt/dtau = hA(t - t_inf) integrated to an exponential decay t = t_inf + (t_f2 - t_inf)exp(-hA/(rho*c_p*V)*tau), giving cooling time T2)

Evaporation heat-loss correction borrowed from Gi-Beum Kim's empirical model: evaporation rate E = (0.37 + 0.0041v)(0.0041*e^(t/22.009) - 0.004)^0.88 (1-phi)^0.88 and latent-heat formula L = (t+273.15)*3.484*t^(-0.174), with total loss Phi_t = Phi_s + Phi_q
CFD numerical simulation in ANSYS Fluent (mesh built in Gambit, ~129,600 cells, Laminar + Energy models) to visualize the 3D/2D temperature field and residual convergence (~379 iterations)
Stefan-Boltzmann radiation check Phi = eps0*A1*C0[((t_f+273.15)/100)^4 - ((t_inf+273.15)/100)^4] to justify neglecting radiation (~2.64% of total)
Person-in-tub skin heat-transfer model Phi_p = (t_f - t_p)/[(1/A3)(delta3/k3 + 1/h3)] treating skin layers as a conduction+convection resistance at 37C body temperature
Bubble-layer insulation model Phi_4 = (t_f - t_inf)/[(1/A4)(1/h4L + delta4/k4 + 1/h4H)] treating the bubble film as an added insulating layer
Geometric shape comparison using AM-GM/average inequality (2*sqrt(pi*A1) <= 4*sqrt(A1) <= perimeter of rectangle) to rank tub shapes by side-surface area / heat loss
Gauss (floor) integral function to count refill iterations for water-consumption accounting m2 = floor(Tb/T + 1)*T1*q_m2
key_techniques: Decomposition into two competing sub-models (continuous vs. discontinuous refill) that are then quantitatively compared on shared criteria, turning a vague 'best strategy' question into an explicit head-to-head
Analogy-driven modeling: the discontinuous model is explicitly framed on the air-conditioner heat/standby duty cycle, a memorable physical intuition that motivates the heating+standby iteration

Pragmatic dimensionality reduction: recognizing the coupled N-S + energy system is analytically intractable (cites the Clay Millennium problem), they hand the field-resolution to CFD while keeping a lumped/mean-temperature analytic model for the headline numbers

Lumped-capacitance simplification (mean temperature t_f = (t_in+t_out)/2) justified by citing that spatial temperature differences are small, which collapses the PDE to tractable algebra/ODEs for the actual answer

Series thermal-resistance network bookkeeping to combine multiple heat-loss pathways (top-surface convection, multi-layer wall conduction, evaporation, body, bubbles, radiation) additively into one total Phi

Correction-as-a-stage: deliberately solving an idealized model first, then bolting on an evaporation correction (using a literature empirical formula) and showing evaporation is actually the dominant loss — a narrative of progressive realism
Heavy one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis with ±2/5/8/12/15% perturbation tables and twin line charts for every factor (volume, person mass, motion via h3, shape), giving percentage elasticities
Using literature parameter ranges with cited sources for every physical constant (skin conductivity, specific heat, convection coefficients) and picking a defensible mid-value
Validation by external benchmark: matching their corrected cooling behavior against Gi-Beum Kim's experimental 250 L tub curves under varying humidity
why_it_won: "From a judge's view this paper wins on completeness-with-discipline rather than mathematical novelty. It does the three things judges reward most: (1) it directly and visibly answers every part of the prompt — space-and-time field (CFD heatmaps), an even/close-to-initial temperature strategy, water minimization, AND all the required influencing factors (shape, volume, person, motion, bubbles) each with its own model and sensitivity table; (2) it makes a decisive, defensible recommendation (continuous low mass-flow refill) backed by a quantitative water-consumption comparison (≈85–98 kg vs ≈113–131 kg), not hand-waving; and (3) it grounds nearly every number in cited literature and validates against a real published experiment (Gi-Beum Kim), so the work feels credible. The air-conditioner analogy and the honest admission that the full N-S system is unsolvable (so CFD is used) signal physical maturity. The layered narrative — idealize, then correct for evaporation, then add the person, then check radiation — reads as a controlled increase in realism that judges can follow. Strong visual communication (flowchart, color temperature fields, a one-page illustrated 'Suggestions/Why' summary for the user) seals the top-tier presentation."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-strong and reusable: Summary -> Background/Literature Review/Problem Restatement -> Assumptions (each individually justified, e.g. radiation neglected via Stefan-Boltzmann + industrial standard) -> Notations table -> Model Overview with a master flowchart -> two parallel sub-models each with Establishment/Results/Simulation -> Correction & Contrast -> Sensitivity Analysis -> Further Discussion -> Strengths/Weaknesses -> a non-technical illustrated 'Report/Suggestions/Why' page for the bather -> References. Standout sections: (1) the Correction & Contrast (sec 7) which both adds evaporation realism and runs the explicit two-criteria comparison that drives the final recommendation; (2) the Sensitivity Analysis (sec 8) which is unusually thorough, giving percentage-change tables plus paired charts for volume, person mass, motion (h3), and tub shape, and even back-checks the radiation assumption; (3) the Further Discussion on multi-faucet inflow, where CFD images of 1/2/5/full-side entrances visually justify the 'more entrances = more even temperature' claim. The closing illustrated suggestions page translates math into five plain actions (add water continuously, smaller tub, fewer motions, use bubbles, more faucets), excellent audience-aware writing."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Internal numerical inconsistencies the judges tolerated but a referee would flag: Sub-model I mass flow is reported as 0.014 in one table and 0.84/0.03 kg/s elsewhere; water-consumption figures slip between 84.6 and 85.18 and 86.4 kg; the recommended final mass flow jumps between 0.03 and 0.16 kg/s across sections

The mean-temperature lumped approximation t_f=(t_in+t_out)/2 partly contradicts the problem's core demand to characterize spatial unevenness; the analytic answer effectively assumes the unevenness away and leans on CFD only for qualitative pictures

CFD results are largely qualitative/illustrative: the discontinuous case is simulated only for 30 s and the standby case is run on a tub shrunk to 1/100 scale with hand-waved inflow velocity (0.04 m/s), so the simulations don't truly resolve the multi-hour dynamics they claim to validate

Equation (39) for evaporation is presented with undefined/garbled symbols and the team ultimately doesn't solve their own derived integral — they instead lift Kim's experimental curve, so the evaporation 'derivation' is decorative

Parameter sourcing is honest but shaky: many constants (skin k3, c_p3, convection coefficients, bubble-layer h/k) are single picks from literature ranges with no propagation of that uncertainty into the results

The person and motion sub-models hold the body-water contact area constant and explicitly skip the surface-area sensitivity 'due to limited time', leaving a known gap in arguably the most important real-world factor

Some assumptions are physically loose (bath water labeled an 'incompressible Non-Newtonian fluid' yet treated with standard Newtonian N-S; evaporation assumed to drop to 10% with bubbles with no support)
reusable_patterns: Two-competing-strategies-then-compare template: build two candidate mechanisms, score both on the same explicit criteria (here mean temperature + water consumption), and let the comparison force a decisive recommendation
Idealize -> correct -> validate pipeline: solve a clean idealized model, add the dominant real-world correction (cite an empirical formula), then validate by matching a published experiment's curve
Series thermal-resistance / additive-flux bookkeeping as a clean way to fold many independent loss channels into one total — directly transferable to any heat/mass/current-balance problem

Lumped-capacitance + 'PDE is intractable, so simulate it' division of labor: keep an analytic closed form for headline numbers, delegate the spatial field to a CFD/FEM tool for visualization (and explicitly cite why the full system can't be solved by hand)
Physical-system analogy to motivate a model (air-conditioner duty cycle -> discontinuous reheating) — a quick credibility and clarity win

Standardized OFAT sensitivity layout: perturb each driver by a fixed ±% ladder, report an elasticity table plus a paired two-axis chart, repeated identically for every factor so judges can compare at a glance

Assumption-with-justification format: every assumption immediately followed by a cited or law-based justification (e.g., Stefan-Boltzmann to drop radiation), and later back-check at least one key assumption numerically (radiation ~2.64%)
Audience-facing deliverable: end with a short, illustrated, jargon-free 'what to do and why' summary aimed at the non-expert stakeholder named in the prompt
